steps.title: Use the contrast
steps.content: The passage first describes a ground-based theory of human bipedalism: an ancestor that practiced knuckle-walking eventually moved upright. The phrase "But recently" introduces orangutan observations that point in a different direction. Orangutans were seen standing on two legs in trees while using their arms for balance and reaching for fruit, so the blank should connect bipedalism to a possible advantage in trees.

steps.title: Reject unsupported claims
steps.content: Choice B goes beyond the evidence by saying bipedalism, knuckle-walking, and tree-climbing must have evolved simultaneously. Choice C shifts to the difficulty of moving between the ground and trees, which the passage never discusses. Choice D applies the orangutan evidence to a knuckle-walking ancestor and claims easy bipedal movement in trees, but the passage does not establish that.

steps.title: Choose the tree-based claim
steps.content: Choice A says bipedalism evolved because it was advantageous to a tree-dwelling ancestor of humans. That fits the orangutan evidence: standing upright in trees while reaching for fruit suggests that upright movement could have helped a tree-dwelling ancestor, offering an alternative to the ground-based knuckle-walking theory. The answer is A.
